title: Nervous System Regulation After Psychic Phenomena

body:
Nervous System Regulation After Psychic Phenomena

You're here, aren't you? It's probably late. You're probably scared. You're probably feeling things you can't explain, things that make you question your sanity, things that have blown your world apart. I get it. I've been there. That buzzing in your head, the electric shocks, the sudden visions, the feeling like you're plugged into something vast and terrifying - yeah, that's familiar. You're not crazy, not in the way they think you are. You're going through something real, something intense, and your nervous system is screaming. My job isn't to tell you what it is exactly - is it kundalini? Is it a spiritual emergency? Is it just your mind playing tricks? I'm not here for labels. I'm here to tell you how to survive it, how to calm the storm that's raging inside you, because that's what I had to do. That's what you need to do. Right?

Look, when you experience things that break the everyday rules of reality - psychic flashes, energy surges, spontaneous movements, auditory phenomena, intense emotional swings that come out of nowhere - your body doesn't know what to do with it. Your nervous system, which is designed to keep you safe from a tiger, suddenly thinks there's a whole damn jungle inside you. It goes into overdrive. Fight, flight, freeze, fawn - you're probably cycling through all of them, sometimes in the space of an hour. You're exhausted, wired, terrified, and totally alone. That's the feeling, isn't it? The isolation is almost as bad as the symptoms themselves. Nobody gets it. They tell you to meditate more, or to see a doctor who just wants to medicate you into oblivion. Neither feels right, because neither addresses the core issue: your system is overwhelmed. It's like you've been given a supercharged engine but your car's chassis is still a bicycle. You need to upgrade the chassis, and that means regulating your nervous system.

The Shock to the System - What's Happening Inside You

So, what exactly is going on? When you have these experiences - whether you call them spiritual awakening, kundalini rising, or just plain weird stuff - it's like a massive download of information and energy. Your brain isn't just processing external stimuli anymore; it's suddenly processing internal, subtle, or even transpersonal energies. It's a lot. Your nervous system, particularly the autonomic nervous system, is caught in the crossfire. You've got the sympathetic nervous system - the gas pedal - stuck on high, constantly pumping adrenaline and cortisol through your body. That's why you feel wired, why your heart races, why you can't sleep, why you're jumpy. Then there's the parasympathetic nervous system - the brake - which is supposed to bring you back down, but it's either unresponsive or it's trying to slam on the brakes so hard you feel completely drained, numb, or dissociated. It's a hell of a ride, and you're not in control. You're just reacting.

I remember lying awake night after night, feeling like my body was vibrating off the bed. My mind was racing, replaying every strange sensation, every unsettling vision. I'd try to meditate, thinking that was the "spiritual" thing to do, but it just made it worse. It was like pouring gasoline on a fire. My system was too agitated to sit still. My body was screaming for movement, for release, but I didn't know how to give it what it needed. I just kept pushing, thinking I had to "get through" it, or "understand" it. But understanding wasn't the problem; regulation was. Your body doesn't care about your spiritual theories; it cares about safety. And right now, it doesn't feel safe. Not at all. Does that land?

The Overactive Alarm Bell

Your amygdala, the brain's alarm center, is basically ringing non-stop. Every strange sensation, every unexplained sound, every vivid dream - it's all interpreted as a threat. And because these experiences are so unusual, so outside your normal frame of reference, your brain doesn't have a file for them. It can't categorize them as "safe" or "normal." So it defaults to "DANGER!" This constant state of alert wears you down. It depletes your resources. You might feel irritable, anxious, depressed, or just plain exhausted. You might find yourself snapping at loved ones or withdrawing completely. This isn't you being "bad" or "failing" at your spiritual path; this is your nervous system doing its job, albeit imperfectly, in a situation it's not equipped to handle. It's a primitive response to an unusual event. Know what I mean?

You might also experience physical symptoms that doctors can't explain: digestive issues, chronic pain, skin rashes, heart palpitations, tremors. These are all signs of a dysregulated nervous system. Your body is trying to tell you something, trying to discharge energy, trying to find a way back to balance. Don't dismiss them as "just in your head." They're real, and they need your attention. I've found a lot of help in books that talk about these kinds of experiences. For instance, The Stormy Search for the Self by Stanislav & Christina Grof (paid link) really opened my eyes to the idea of spiritual emergencies and how complex they can be. It's not just you; this is a known, albeit rarely discussed, phenomenon.

Practical Steps for Grounding and Calming

Okay, so you know what's happening. Now, what do you do about it? Forget trying to "fix" the spiritual stuff right now. Your first priority is to bring your body back to a place of relative safety. You can't process anything deeper if you're constantly in fight-or-flight. Here's what worked for me, and what I've seen work for countless others.

  • Movement, but the Right Kind: When your system is agitated, it often wants to discharge energy. Running or intense cardio might feel good for a bit, but it can also keep that sympathetic nervous system revved up. Try gentle, rhythmic movements. Walking in nature, especially with bare feet on the earth. Slow, mindful stretching. Qigong or gentle Tai Chi. Even just shaking your body out, literally shaking your limbs like a dog shaking off water. This helps release stored tension and trauma.
  • Sensory Input for Safety: Your senses are your connection to the present moment. Engage them deliberately. What can you see, hear, smell, taste, touch right now? Drink a warm, comforting tea and really feel the warmth, taste the flavor. Hold an ice cube in your hand and focus on the sensation. Listen to grounding music - not necessarily relaxing spa music, but something with a steady beat that feels solid. Look at something beautiful and complex, like a plant or a piece of art, and just observe it without judgment. These small acts anchor you.
  • Connect with the Earth: I can't stress this enough. Get outside. Put your bare feet on the grass, the dirt, the sand. Sit with your back against a tree. Feel the wind on your skin, the sun on your face. This isn't woo-woo; it's a direct way to discharge excess energy and reconnect with something stable and vast. It reminds your system that you're part of something bigger than your internal chaos.
  • Hydration and Nutrition: You're probably not eating or drinking enough, or you're reaching for comfort foods that actually make you feel worse. Your brain and nervous system need good fuel. Water, electrolytes, nutrient-dense foods. Cut out caffeine, sugar, and anything that makes you feel more jittery. This isn't about dieting; it's about giving your body the building blocks it needs to repair itself.
  • Breathwork, Carefully: Deep breathing can be amazing, but if your system is completely dysregulated, some breathwork can actually trigger more anxiety. Start simple. Focus on extending your exhale. Breathe in for a count of 4, hold for 2, exhale for 6. Or just focus on exhaling completely, letting all the stale air out. The exhale activates the parasympathetic nervous system. Don't force it. If it feels worse, stop.
  • Sound and Vibration: Humming, chanting, or listening to low-frequency sounds can be incredibly soothing. The vagus nerve, a key player in nervous system regulation, runs through your throat and is stimulated by vocalizations. Try humming quietly to yourself. Or put on some binaural beats designed for delta or theta brainwaves, but again, if it feels off, stop.
  • Boundaries, Boundaries, Boundaries: You're probably more sensitive than ever. Protect your energy. Limit exposure to stressful news, people who drain you, loud environments, or anything that feels overwhelming. It's okay to say no. It's okay to retreat. This isn't selfish; it's self-preservation.

You might find books like The Kundalini Experience by Lee Sannella (paid link) helpful, not necessarily for advice on what to do, but just to feel less alone, to see that others have gone through similar things. It normalizes what feels utterly abnormal.

Finding Support - You Don't Have to Do This Alone

This is probably the hardest part. Because who do you talk to? Your friends and family might not understand. A typical therapist might pathologize you. A spiritual teacher might tell you to just "surrender" or "raise your vibration" - which, honestly, can be infuriating when you're just trying not to spontaneously combust. Here's what I've learned about finding support.

  • Look for Trauma-Informed Practitioners: Even if your experience isn't "trauma" in the traditional sense, the nervous system response is very similar. Therapists who specialize in trauma, especially those who use modalities like Somatic Experiencing (SE) or Internal Family Systems (IFS), understand nervous system regulation. They won't necessarily understand the spiritual content, but they can help you feel safe in your body again, which is half the battle.
  • Find a Spiritual Emergency Network: There are organizations and communities specifically for people going through these kinds of experiences. They're rare, but they exist. They can connect you with people who get it, and sometimes with practitioners who are open to these kinds of phenomena.
  • Connect with Others Who Get It: Online forums, support groups - be discerning, but finding even one person who says, "Yeah, I've felt that too," can be a lifesaver. Just make sure you're not getting pulled into more fear or dogma. You need validation, not more confusion.
  • Be Honest, But Guarded: When you do talk to people, whether it's a doctor or a friend, you don't have to tell them everything. You can describe your physical symptoms - the anxiety, the sleeplessness, the physical sensations - without getting into the "I saw auras" or "I felt a serpent moving up my spine" stuff if you think they won't understand. Your priority is getting the support you need, not educating everyone.

The truth is, this journey is incredibly isolating. I remember feeling like I was screaming into a void. But there are people who understand, even if they're hard to find. Books like When Spirit Leaps: Navigating the Process of Spiritual Awakening by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link) or The Kundalini Guide by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link) can be like a friend in the middle of the night, offering a sense of shared experience and a roadmap for what you're going through. You're not alone in the dark.

Patience, Compassion, and Time - The Unsung Healers

Here's the thing: this isn't a quick fix. You didn't get into this state overnight, and you won't get out of it overnight. It takes time. A lot of time. And during that time, you need to cultivate immense patience and compassion for yourself. You're going through something incredibly challenging, something that most people will never experience. You're basically rewiring your entire system. That's a huge undertaking.

You'll have good days and bad days. You'll feel like you're making progress, and then you'll crash. That's normal. Don't beat yourself up for it. Don't judge yourself for not being "spiritual enough" or "strong enough." Just keep coming back to the basics: grounding, breathing, movement, safety. Each time you gently guide your nervous system back towards balance, you're building new pathways. You're teaching your body that it's okay, that it can survive this. You're building resilience.

I remember feeling so angry at my body, at whatever had "happened" to me. I just wanted it to stop. But that anger just added more fuel to the fire. It wasn't until I started treating myself with kindness, like I would a scared child, that things slowly began to shift. I started acknowledging the fear, the pain, the confusion, without trying to push it away. I started saying, "Okay, this is happening. What can I do right now to make myself feel even 1% safer?" That small shift in perspective made all the difference. So yeah, be kind to yourself. You deserve it.

Reclaiming Your Agency

One of the scariest parts of these experiences is the feeling of losing control. Your body feels like it's doing its own thing, your mind is racing, and you feel like a passenger in your own life. Reclaiming your agency, even in small ways, is powerful. Choose what you eat. Choose when you rest. Choose where you spend your time. Choose to engage in a grounding practice, even if it's just for five minutes. These choices, however small, remind your system that you are still in charge, that you have a say in your well-being. It's not about forcing things, but about making conscious, supportive choices for yourself.

You're not broken. You're not defective. You're simply going through an intense process that requires a different kind of care than you're used to. Your nervous system is trying to adapt to a new reality, and it needs your help. It needs your patience. It needs your unwavering compassion. You'll get through this. It won't always feel like this. The storm will pass, and you'll emerge stronger, more resilient, and with a deeper understanding of yourself than you ever thought possible. Keep breathing. Keep grounding. You're doing better than you think. I promise.

This is not a substitute for professional mental health care. If you're in crisis, please reach out to a qualified therapist or call 988.
author: Kalesh
